reason: The statement does apply to the text fragment as it describes a king, Saul, who rises to power and carries out violent actions against a specific group of people, in this case, the Amalekites. Saul is instructed by the LORD to utterly destroy the Amalekites, including men, women, children, and animals. However, Saul disobeys and spares the king, Agag, as well as the best of the livestock. This disobedience leads to his rejection as king by the LORD, showing the consequences of his actions.
